title: Colonial Legacy and Informal Finance
abstract: An influential line of research emphasizes that colonial legacy plays a key role in formal financial development. Can colonial legacy also shape informal finance? We investigate the impact of colonial legacy on informal financial development using a manually georeferenced data set within a credible empirical framework. In the 19th century, Europeans arbitrarily designed colonial borders that partitioned many ethnicities across multiple countries in Africa. Leveraging several spatial regression discontinuity designs across national borders and within British-French–partitioned Cameroon and a unique natural experiment where the same former British colony is compared with two otherwise similar areas with different exposures to French colonization, we discover that former British colonies today have better informal financial development than former French colonies. Exploring the channels, we find that places with a British colonial legacy maintain a style of social control that facilitates information flow, supports private enforcement and market interactions, and promotes strong legal cultures.

author.Jiafu An: Department of Real Estate and Construction, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR, China
author.Chen Lin: Faculty of Business and Economics, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR, China
author.Mingzhu Tai: Faculty of Business and Economics, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR, China

article.1. Introduction: Research shows that colonial legacy has a long-lasting impact on financial development today.La Porta et al. (1997,1998), for instance, document that European powers imposed different legal traditions on their colonies centuries ago, and these legal traditions differ in the extent to which they protect the rights of private investors against the state, with important implications for modern financial development (Cull and Xu 2005,Brown et al. 2017). In another influential line of work,Acemoglu et al. (2001,2002) show that the colonization strategies adopted by the European colonizers varied greatly because of the disease environment they encountered. This led to the establishment of extractive institutions in places with poor feasibility of settlement and, in other areas (i.e., Neo-Europes), strong property rights protection and contract enforcement, with significant ramifications on financial development today (Beck et al. 2003). Although these studies provide valuable insights, they have predominantly centered on the impact of colonial legacy on formal financial markets and offer limited evidence on whether and how colonial legacy can influence the development of informal finance. This is surprising, especially when considering that nearly half of the global population is unbanked (Demirgüç-Kunt et al. 2018). Informal finance, defined as financial transactions conducted outside the purview of intermediaries subject to banking and financial regulations and not relying on the state for enforcing legal contractual obligations, serves as a crucial means for the unbanked to access external financial resources.1 Does colonial legacy impact informal finance development? One perspective suggests that former British colonies benefited from a legal culture emphasizing private economic interactions and the rule of law (La Porta et al. 2008), which fostered informal financial development at early stages. Another viewpoint contrasts France’s centralized colonization approach with Britain’s divide-and-rule strategy, indicating that the British method promoted strong in-group prosocial behaviors and enhanced community-based information sharing (Acemoglu et al. 2014,Ali et al. 2019)—both crucial for the development of informal finance. This raises a pertinent question: Did the British colonial legacy differently promote modern informal financial development compared with the French colonial legacy? Estimating the causal impact of colonial legacy on informal financial development is, however, challenging. Countries with different colonial histories often differ in many other dimensions, such as their institutions, culture, geography, and politics, which can help explain the variation in both formal and informal financial development (D’Acunto 2015,Brown et al. 2019,Brown et al. 2019,D’Acunto et al. 2019,Gao et al. 2021). A cross-country comparison, therefore, is likely to be confounded by omitted variables (Musacchio and Turner 2013,Levine et al. 2020). In addition, the lack of disaggregated data on informal finance at the subnational level further hinders researchers from designing credible identification strategies to examine the causal link between colonial legacy and informal finance. This study contributes to the literature that evaluates the impact of colonial legacy on modern finance by estimating the causal effect of colonial institutions on modern informal financial development. To do so, we take advantage of the artificial drawing of African borders and a manually georeferenced data set obtained from the FinMark Trust on household finance, which jointly allows us to overcome the key empirical challenge: omitted variable bias. In the 19th century, when the European powers had barely set foot in Africa, they arbitrarily designed colonial boundaries that partitioned nearly 200 ethnicities across multiple countries (Michalopoulos and Papaioannou 2014,2016). We leverage this quasi-natural experiment by comparing informal financial development across regions that fall into the same ethnic homeland but belong to different countries with different colonial histories. This empirical setting allows us to hold geo-climate conditions and ethnicity-specific cultural traits fixed when studying the link between colonial origins and informal finance, thus mitigating the omitted variable bias. To further strengthen our identification strategy, we zoom in and perform a similar near-border comparison in Cameroon, the only African country that was arbitrarily divided between Britain and France after World War I (WWI) and was subsequently reunited after gaining independence in 1960. This within-country but across-different-colonizers comparison allows us to eliminate the omitted variable bias at the country level, where colonial institutions normally vary, thus isolate the impact of colonial legacy on informal financial development. Finally, the unique history of Cameroon creates a novel setting in which the same control group (i.e., regions in bordering Nigeria, a former British colony) can be compared with two otherwise similar treatment groups with different levels of treatment exposure (i.e., historically British- and French-colonized regions of Cameroon), which adds strength to our identification strategy.2 Several key features of the British colonization approach are critical to understand the mechanisms that link colonial legacy to informal financial development today (Beck and Levine 2005,La Porta et al. 2008,Ali et al. 2019,McNamee 2019). As recently stressed byAllen et al. (2019)and others, for any financing channel to be effective, it needs to address adverse selection and moral hazard problems. The divide-and-rule colonization approach adopted by Britain in Africa greatly strengthened ethnic identity over the national identity, which in turn sustained strong in-group prosocial norms conducive to community-based information sharing and flow (Bowles 2006,Choi and Bowles 2007,Bauer et al. 2014). This helps address ex ante adverse selection issues and ex post moral hazard problems (Padilla and Pagano 2000).3Thus, we conjecture and empirically test whether people in former British colonies are more willing to exchange information than those in regions previously colonized by France. Secondly, local chiefs were greatly empowered by the British to allocate key resources among their respective populations. This suggests that chiefs could effectively enforce contracts by threatening to abrogate the access right to vital resources or expulsion, which helps deal with recourse in case of default and curb ex post moral hazard. To assess this channel, we investigate the effect of British colonization on people’s impression of chiefs today. Lastly, as emphasized byLa Porta et al. (2008), Britain brought common law tradition and the associated style of social control to its colonies. Such an approach to social control favored private economic transactions and was accompanied by legal principles that stressed secure private property rights and investor protection. Although specific legal rules have evolved over time, these principles have been embedded in people’s beliefs, and created, in the words of La Portaet al. (2008, p. 309), a “legal culture” that respects and trusts the judiciary. In contrast, such legal culture is absent from the civil law tradition, which favored state-orientation and centralization, substantially suppressing the power of judiciary (Glaeser and Shleifer 2002). To empirically examine this aspect, we assess whether British colonization is positively correlated with (a) levels of trust in courts and respect for court decisions and (b) measures of private market interactions, both within and across countries. We combine a georeferenced survey data set on household finance in Africa obtained from FinMark Trust, the Global Findex database (2011–2017), and the Afrobarometer (2016), to investigate the causal impact of colonial legacy on informal financial development and channels of influence. The combination of these datasets presents the first opportunity to systematically study the links between colonization and informal financial markets at a disaggregated level. The household surveys obtained from the FinMark Trust (2005–2019) provide information on survey locations and respondents’ financial issues, as well as various demographic characteristics of 24 countries in Africa. We manually map the survey locations, based on the coordinates of the locations’ centroids, to the ethnic group map of Africa ofMurdock (1967), as well as to the current national boundaries on the continent. This matching procedure, combined with the quasi-natural experimental design of the artificial drawing of borders, generates an empirical setting known as a spatial regression discontinuity design (RDD). This allows us to compare informal financial outcomes across places in very close proximity but with different colonizers, both within and across countries. Our key outcome variable is whether the respondent uses informal financial services, including savings, credit, and insurance, provided by informal financial groups. Although the Global Findex data set (2011–2017) covers all African countries, it lacks information on respondents’ locations. We therefore use it only to motivate our key empirical analyses. To preview our results, we find that households in former British colonies are more likely to be included in the informal financial system today than their observably similar counterparts in former French colonies. This result is obtained from cross-national-border regressions that compare the informal financial outcomes of respondents who live in the same ethnic homelands, in very close proximity (within 55 × 55-km2cells), but in countries with different colonial institutions (i.e., British versus French). We obtain similar results when further including a range of demographic controls, such as a polynomial of age, gender, education, income source, and income quintile fixed effects. The economic impact of colonial legacy on informal finance is large. Based on the estimate from our most restrictive model, within the same ethnic homeland, if a group of people moves from a location in a former French colony to a location about 50 km away, in a former British colony, their probability of being included in the informal financial system increases by 19.5 percentage points, which is more than a third of our sample mean (0.55).4 Although our cross-country-border analyses have controlled for the potential influence of other aspects of culture, geography, and climate through including ethnicity and grid cell fixed effects, we are concerned about potential omitted variables arising from the difference in national policies. For example, if former French-colonized countries, because of their state orientation and centralization, are better at designing and/or implementing policies that promote informal financial inclusion than former British colonies in general, then our estimates could be biased downward. To address this concern, we zoom in and focus on Cameroon, the only African country that was arbitrarily divided and colonized by Britain and France. During WWI, the British and French invaded the country, a then German colony (Kamerun), and partitioned the country into two administrative regions in 1919. After about 40 years of separate colonization, the two regions were reunited in 1961 after Cameroon gained independence.5As the country was striving for complete political and legal unification, a unitary legal system that heavily relies on French institutions was created in 1972 and continues to operate today with some amendments. This setting provides a unique opportunity to hold all country-level institutions constant when comparing informal financial development in locations just across the 1919 border within Cameroon.6This result confirms the positive impact of British, relative to French, colonial legacy on informal financial development and remains robust even when we further control for a smooth function of geographic location in a spatial regression discontinuity framework (Dell 2010). The economic magnitude of the result is substantial. Using our most restrictive model, for example, if a group of people moves from the French side of Cameroon to an area about 50 km away on the British side, their probability of using informal finance will jump by 40.4 percentage points. We next perform a plethora of sensitivity tests, including those focusing on various bandwidths around the 1919 border, using different smooth functions of geographic location and applying various specific assumptions about the variance-covariance matrix. Our estimates continue to hold across all specifications. We also conduct an exercise in which we randomly draw a placebo border within a ±20-km band of the original boundary and rerun our test 1,000 times. When tabulating the estimates from this exercise in a probability density graph, we find that the baseline coefficient of British colonization is at the far end of the right tail (less than 1%). This suggests that the within-Cameroon results are unlikely to be spurious because of measurement errors of the historical boundary. We then conduct three additional analyses to mitigate the possibility that the within-country results are driven by cross-border migrations within Cameroon. First, because of the high cost of transportation in African inland regions and the stable linkages between ethnic group identity and access to basic production factors (e.g., land and water) in Cameroon (Ali et al. 2019), we hypothesize that much of the selective sorting on informal financial markets, if it exists, is concentrated on a narrow band along the border. We therefore exclude observations that fall within a ±10-km band of the border and rerun our analysis. The results remain robust and become even stronger. Second, following the approach ofDell (2010), we recode 10% of the informal finance users on the British side to nonusers, and vice versa, in the formerly French controlled region, given thatDupraz (2019)estimates that average cross-border in- and out-migration (relative to the British side) ranges from 4% to 9%. This conservative sample, fully attributing cross-border migration to selective sorting on informal finance, generates estimates similar to our baseline results.7Next, we conduct spatial RDDs at the Nigeria-Cameroon border, because moving across countries is much more costly than moving within national boundaries.8The results from this set of tests, again, confirm our baseline estimates. Lastly, the Nigeria-Cameroon border presents an opportunity to conduct a novel three-group comparison with two treatment arms, analogous to a randomized controlled trial where researchers randomly vary the level of treatment exposure in two otherwise similar treatment groups (Duflo and Banerjee 2017). In particular, because of Cameroon’s unique colonial history, one part of the Nigeria-Cameroon border separates Nigeria from French Cameroon, whereas another adjoins Nigeria and British Cameroon. This is conceptually similar to a setting in which the same control group (i.e., respondents in Nigeria) is compared with two otherwise similar groups with different levels of treatment exposure (i.e., respondents in British versus French Cameroon). In other words, we can isolate the causal impact of legal origins by simultaneously comparing informal finance outcomes in regions that had always been under British colonization (i.e., Nigeria) with those in (a) a place that had always been under French colonization (i.e., the French-colonized Cameroon) and (b) a region that had initially been under British control but was then converted to a French colony because of unification (i.e., the British-colonized Cameroon). If our British colonization indicator captures the impact of other factors that shape the differential trajectories of informal financial development in Nigeria and Cameroon, then we expect similar results when analyzing the two parts of the border separately. However, if our analysis captures the true impact of British colonial legacy, we expect some heterogeneity in the estimates. Our findings reveal that the impact of British colonization, estimated at the Nigeria-French Cameroon border, is quantitatively comparable to our baseline results, whereas the effect at the border dividing Nigeria and British Cameroon is substantially smaller than the size of our previous estimate.9This implies that the positive impact of British colonial legacy on informal financial markets is not completely driven by omitted variables. Why does colonial legacy have such a profound impact on the development of informal finance today? And why does the impact remain persistent and large, even when we make within-country comparisons in which all contemporary national institutions are held constant? We test three channels that link colonial legacy to informal finance: information-sharing norms, the legacy of the style of social control, and legal culture. Firstly, we discover that places colonized by the British have sustained a better community-based information flow. In particular, people in former British colonies exhibit a greater tendency to keep social ties and willingness to exchange ideas. The logic is that stronger social ties coupled with a higher propensity to share information can jointly promote better information flow. The economic significance of this impact is nontrivial. If we were to move a group of people from the historically British-colonized region to a French-ruled region within Cameroon, the probability of their willingness to exchange information would jump by nearly 30 percentage points, representing more than a 100% increase when evaluated at the sample mean. In addition, as a result of the British style of social control, which emphasizes private interactions, people are more likely to form private safety nets providing mutual economic insurance, attend community meetings, and join other social groups in the former British colonies. Relatedly, perhaps as a legacy of local leadership administrations, people in former British colonies tend to have a better impression of their traditional leaders and a higher level of trust in private groups. Finally, people in former British colonies place more trust in courts of law and show more respect for court decisions. Even within Cameroon, where the de jure legal system is the same today, people in the regions colonized by the British during the period 1919–1960 still place more confidence in the judiciary than their otherwise similar counterparts living in French-colonized places. Many of these results are obtained from both cross- and within-country analyses, and their economic magnitudes are similar to, if not greater than, the magnitude we estimated on legal culture. This paper differs from and complements the existing literature that studies the link between colonial legacy and financial development. Since the seminal work byLa Porta et al. (1997,1998,2008),Acemoglu et al. (2001,2002), and other scholars, the idea that colonial legacy has shaped financial development through institutions has received ample empirical support from various cross-country studies (Levine 1999,2005;La Porta et al. 2002;Beck et al. 2003;Djankov et al. 2007,2008;Brown et al. 2013). However, this line of research has focused overwhelmingly on the development of formal financial markets, such as the banking system and the equity market, and provides little systematic evidence on informal finance. Despite the importance of formal finance in promoting growth (Cong et al. 2019), it is crucial to also understand what shapes informal financial development because a large share of the world’s population is unbanked and therefore can benefit from it. Our paper offers causal evidence on (a) the positive impact of British (versus French) colonial legacy on informal financial development and (b) the channels that link colonial legacy to informal finance today. Our results are also relevant to policymakers who aim to increase the provision of financial services to the poor and help build their financial resilience. For example, based on our evidence, policies that encourage information flow, empower the private enforcement of contracts, and/or sustain a strong legal culture (i.e., trust in courts, respect for judges, etc.), can be conducive to the development of informal finance.
article.2. Institutional Setting: Is the colonial past influential in shaping informal finance today? Did the impact of British colonialism on modern informal financial development differ significantly from that of French colonialism? One view contrasts France’s centralized colonization approach with Britain’s divide-and-rule strategy, where populations were separated along tribal lines and were ruled indirectly by local chiefs (Acemoglu et al. 2014,Ali et al. 2019).10Research indicates that the British approach promoted stronger ethnic identity and facilitated information sharing through in-group prosocial behaviors (Ali et al. 2019,McNamee 2019,An et al. 2022). This feature is a crucial prerequisite for informal financial development, as a better information environment enhances screening, reduces adverse selection, and mitigates moral hazard through monitoring and the potential for deterrence (Pagano and Jappelli 1993,Padilla and Pagano 2000,Allen et al. 2019). France, on the other hand, emphasized state centralization, which suppressed the power of local chiefs and limited private market interactions. Furthermore, Britain’s indirect rule empowered local chiefs to allocate essential resources such as land and water within their populations. This feature suggests that local chiefs could effectively enforce informal contracts by leveraging access to vital productive resources or the threat of expulsion. It is important to note that the impacts of the divide-and-rule strategy on finance mainly affect informal financial markets, because community-based information flow and enforcement cannot substitute for public credit registries or legal enforcement, which are crucial for the development of formal financial markets. Another perspective suggests former British colonies inherited a style of social control emphasizing private economic interactions and the rule of law. This legal culture, as described byLa Porta et al. (2008), is believed to have positive implications for financial development. Note that the benefits of the British social control style can apply to both informal and formal financial development but tend to manifest at different development stages. Informal financial markets, operating on a small scale and not relying on judicial enforcement, may benefit at the early developing stages in the absence of well-functioning formal institutions. In contrast, the positive impact on formal finance may emerge later when there is a reasonably well-functioning legal system for enforcing contractual legal obligations (Djankov et al. 2003), a relatively large public/private credit bureau system for screening and pricing financial products (Pagano and Jappelli 1993), and sufficient formal financial inclusion facilitated by strong state capacity and/or technology, which allows for the collection and recording of credit histories for individuals and firms (Jack and Suri 2014).11 The key features discussed above suggest three main channels via which colonialism shapes informal financial development. Although the first two traits of the British colonization strategy (i.e., strong in-group prosocial norms and empowered chiefs) mainly promote informal financial development because of their community-based nature, the legal culture feature can have positive impacts on both formal and informal financial development at different growth stages. First, Britain’s flexible approach to social control in Africa, which divided the population along tribal lines and was administered through local chiefs, was associated with more private and community interactions than the centralized style of control adopted by France (Acemoglu et al. 2014). This, in turn, supported a greater information flow in the former British colonies. As emphasized by an influential line of research, more information can (a) mitigate the problems of adverse selection in the informal credit market (Allen et al. 2019) by enabling lenders to target better (Pagano and Jappelli 1993,Djankov et al. 2007); and (b) reduce moral hazards by facilitating disciplinary actions (Padilla and Pagano 2000,Barth et al. 2009). This, in turn, can lead to better informal financial development. Furthermore, the British divide-and-rule strategy in Africa delegated the power of contract enforcement and administration to local chiefs (Acemoglu et al. 2014,Ali et al. 2019). With little knowledge of African inland states, Britain’s flexible approach to social control in Africa substantially reduced the cost and increased the efficacy of administration,12in contrast to the centralized approach adopted by France, which significantly depressed the powers of local chiefs. As a result, chiefs in former British colonies could effectively enforce both formal and informal financial contracts by leveraging the access rights to vital resources and the threat of expulsion. Therefore, informal financial groups, whose sustainability depends on private enforcement (Ayyagari et al. 2010), are more likely to emerge and persist in the former British colonies. Such benefits do not accrue to the formal finance because the effective enforcement of formal financial contracts mainly relies on a well-functioned judiciary. Lastly, in former British colonies, the common law style to social control has fostered a culture marked by relatively high levels of trust and confidence in the legal system. This legal culture may persist to this day, yielding positive effects on both private market outcomes and financial contracting. It is worth noting that such a legal culture can benefit both formal and informal finance. Informal finance can develop because of active private economic transactions, whereas formal finance can reap the benefits when supported by a well-functioning judiciary for the enforcement of laws and contracts (Djankov et al. 2003), a substantial credit registry for loan screening and pricing (Pagano and Jappelli 1993), and a sizeable banked population (Jack and Suri 2014). Conversely, in French civil law colonies, the approach of social control favored state-centralization, significantly limiting the influence of the judiciary. This approach may have hindered the development of a legal culture that trusts and respects the courts, with negative repercussions for private economic activities and modern finance. It is important to stress that the channels through which colonial legacy influences informal financial development do not operate in isolation. Enhanced localized information flows, which significantly reduce ex ante information asymmetry, combined with a cultural inclination toward private economic dealings, are conducive to the initiation of informal financial contracts. Britain’s divide-and-rule colonization strategy provides strong, private enforcement of informal contracts helping to sustain these groups. In the absence of effective social deterrents, enforced by local authorities to minimize moral hazards, sustaining informal financial groups becomes challenging. Additionally, in former British colonies, the high level of information exchange, spurred by regular private and communal interactions, not only alleviates the risk of adverse selection but also bolsters the effectiveness of private enforcement. This, in turn, substantially aids the growth and development of informal financial markets.
article.3. Data: We manually georeference a new household data set, obtained from FinMark Trust (2005–2019), and combine it with the Afrobarometer (2016) to study the impact of colonial legacy on informal finance (An 2020). FinMark Trust is an independent nonprofit trust that aims to promote financial inclusion and regional financial integration in developing countries. Since 2005, the Trust has conducted a series of nationally representative household surveys across 24 countries on the African mainland.13Apart from respondents’ locations, the survey also provides information on respondents’ demographics including age, gender, education, income, and so on, and various financial behaviors such as saving, borrowing, and the use of insurance. Particularly relevant to our study, the survey contains data on whether respondents use informal finance or not and the reasons behind the decision. To examine the channels through which colonial legacy influences informal finance, we combine the FinMark Trust data with the 2016 Afrobarometer surveys. The Afrobarometer, a pan-African research institution, provides individual-level nationally representative surveys on people’s attitudes toward many aspects of the society, including trust in the judiciary and other institutions, subjective evaluations of traditional leaders, beliefs about private interactions, and so on. We obtained a version of the Afrobarometer surveys that contain precise survey locations, which enables us to match the surveys with FinMark Trust and national boundaries. We also use the Global Findex data set (2011–2017) to motivate our key empirical analyses. The Global Findex data set, maintained by the World Bank, contains individual-level data, aggregated at the country level, on the usage of various financial services from formal financial institutions, mobile banking systems, and savings clubs. The data set also provides some information on the reasons why people are not included in the formal financial system. Our key innovation is to map the new household data set to the ethnic group map of Africa ofMurdock (1967), as well as current national boundaries on the continent. This procedure produces a disaggregated data set on informal finance that allows us to apply a novel empirical strategy, which can substantially mitigate the omitted variable bias that has long plagued the literature on colonial legacy and financial development. When studying the mechanisms that relate colonial legacy to the development of informal finance, we further combine this matched data set with the georeferenced Afrobarometer (2016). Although the Global Findex data set covers all African countries, it does not contain information on respondents’ locations; thus, we use it only to motivate our key empirical analyses. The remainder of this section describes the data and variables used in our analyses. Because our key data sets are household or individual surveys, our unit of analysis is respondents. We present detailed variable definitions and sources inTable 1and summary statistics inTable 2. Table 1.Key Variable Definitions and Data Sources Table 1.Key Variable Definitions and Data Sources Table 2.Summary Statistics Table 2.Summary Statistics 3.1. Measure of Colonial Legacy In our cross-country examinations, the explanatory variable,British, is an indicator that equals one if the respondent is from a country with a British colonial legacy, and zero otherwise. We classify a country’s colonial legacy based onDjankov et al. (2007). In our within-country analyses,Britishis equal to one if the respondent is from an area that was previously colonized by the British and zero otherwise. As shown inTable 2, on average, 21% of respondents included in FinMark Trust surveys are from places that were colonized by the British, whereas 40% and 42% of respondents are from former British colonies in the Afrobarometer and the Global Findex database, respectively. 3.2. Measures of Informal Financial Development Our key measure of informal financial development,Informal finance, is an indicator that equals one if the respondent uses informal finance groups for saving, borrowing, and insurance, and zero otherwise. We obtain this measure from the new database, FinMark Trust household surveys. Because we require (a) information on survey locations to conduct spatial RDDs and (b) adjacent countries to have different legal origins, only 10 of the 19 countries end up in our sample. We then project the survey locations onto the ethnic homeland map of Africa ofMurdock (1967)using manually collected coordinates of the survey locations’ centroids. In total, our sample contains more than 60,000 respondents.Figure 1visualizes the sample coverage from FinMark Trust. The colored polygons represent the ethnic homelands that have been partitioned by the British-French national borders. As presented inTable 2, about 55% of respondents in our sample use informal finance. The standard deviation of 0.5 suggests that there is a substantial amount of variation inInformal financein our sample. Focusing on respondents in Cameroon, about 45% use informal finance, again with a standard deviation of 0.5. We use an alternative measure of informal finance,Saving club, in a test that motivates our main analyses.14It is an indicator that equals one if the respondent belongs to a saving club, and zero otherwise. This measure is obtained from the Global Findex database (2011–2017) and is available for all African countries. As shown inTable 2, about 21% of Africans use savings clubs, with a standard deviation of 0.41. Note that this is likely an underestimate of informal financial development because saving clubs can only represent a part of informal finance. 3.3. Mechanism Indicators We examine the style of social control that supports private market interactions, legal culture, and information sharing as three potential mechanisms through which colonial legacy affects informal financial development in Africa. We consider three sets of measures that reflect the style of social control favoring private market outcomes. The first set, obtained from FinMark Trust, contains two variables, that is,Safety netandTrust in informal groups. We can only use these variables in the within-Cameroon analysis because of (a) the lack of this information in some countries and/or (b) the lack of adjacent countries with different colonizers. Specifically,Safety netequals one if the respondent believes that they can get help from members of informal financial groups when needed and zero otherwise. Similarly,Trust in informal groupsis an indicator that equals one if the respondent states that the reason for joining informal financial groups is their trust in the group members and zero otherwise. The second set of measures is obtained from the Afrobarometer, includingAttend community meetings, Voluntary associations,andCommunity safety. Because these variables are available for all countries in our sample,15we can perform both cross- and within-country spatial RDDs. In particular,Attend community meetingsis an indicator that equals one if the respondent attends community meetings, and zero otherwise;Voluntary associationsis a dummy variable that equals one if the respondent is an active member/leader of a voluntary association, and zero otherwise;Community safetyis assigned a value of one if the respondent feels safe when walking in the community, and zero otherwise. Relatedly, because the colonization approach adopted by the British in Africa was through local chiefs, we construct a measure that reflects this legacy. In particular,Traditional leaderis an indicator that equals one if the respondent approves of the way in which the traditional leader has performed their job over the past 12 months, and zero otherwise. In sum, these six measures jointly paint a picture that reflects the extent to which the style of social control favors private economic interactions. As shown inTable 2, there is a large number of in-sample variations in these measures. We construct two measures of legal culture from the Afrobarometer.Trust courts of lawis an indicator that equals one if the respondent trusts courts of law, and zero otherwise;Courts make binding decisionsis a dichotomous variable that equals one if the respondent agrees with the statement that courts make binding decisions, and zero otherwise. Again, because these variables are available for all the countries in our sample, we can examine the relationship between colonial legacy and measures of legal culture using both within-country and cross-country spatial RDDs. The summary statistics presented inTable 2suggest that these measures vary substantially across respondents, with a standard deviation of 0.38 forTrust courts of lawand 0.43 forCourts make binding decisions. Finally, we obtain two measures that capture the extent of information flow from the FinMark Trust. Specifically,Exchange ideasequals one if the respondent states that the reason for using informal finance is to exchange ideas with other group members, and zero otherwise;Keep social tiesis an indicator that equals one if the respondent states that the reason for using informal finance is to maintain social ties and zero otherwise. The idea is that stronger social ties coupled with a higher propensity to share information can jointly reflect better information flow. As shown inTable 2,Exchange ideasandKeep social tieshave sample means of 0.19 and 0.18, whereas their standard deviations are 0.39 and 0.38, respectively. These statistics suggest a high level of in-sample variation. 3.4. Other Respondents’ Characteristics When examining the link between colonial legacy and informal financial development, we control for respondent demographics, including age (Age), age squared (Age squared), and gender (Female), along with a range of fixed effects regarding respondents’ levels of education, income, and living conditions. Specifically,Educationin the Afrobarometer sample, contains 10 indicators of the respondent’s education categories: (1) no formal schooling, (2) informal schooling only, (3) some primary schooling, (4) primary school completed, (5) some secondary school/high school, (6) secondary school completed/high school, (7) postsecondary qualifications, but no university, (8) some university, (9) university completed, and (10) postgraduate. In the FinMark Trust sample,Educationincludes six indicators of the respondent’s education categories: (1) no formal education, (2) primary education, (3) secondary education, (4) vocational/specialized training, (5) tertiary education, and (6) other.Income, obtained from FinMark Trust, contains four indicators for within-country household income quantiles. Because the Afrobarometer does not contain information on respondents’ income levels, we useLiving conditionsinstead. It includes five indicators of the respondent’s view of their present living conditions: (1) very bad, (2) fairly bad, (3) neither good nor bad, (4) fairly good, or (5) very good. As presented inTable 2, respondents in our sample are typically middle-aged, with most of them in their 30 s. About half of the respondents are female. The median respondent has completed primary education and has a middle-class income in their respective country. We next examine the impact of colonial legacy on informal financial development, using the data described here.
article.4. Colonial Legacy and Informal Financia: In this section, we examine the relationship between colonial legacy and informal financial development in a novel empirical framework. In the mid- to late 19th century, the colonial powers in European capitals arbitrarily designed colonial boundaries in Africa, partitioning nearly 200 ethnicities across multiple countries (Michalopoulos and Papaioannou 2014,2016). Our key identification strategy exploits this historical fact to compare the outcomes of informal finance across regions in very close proximity, belonging to the same ethnic homeland, but with different colonial legacies (i.e., British versus French). The boundaries that we are studying were largely arbitrarily drawn. Europeans in most cases did not even set foot in the regions that they were trying to divide during the Scramble for Africa. The key considerations for the partitioning was to preserve the status quo preventing conflicts among the Europeans. As a result, European drew borders without considering local conditions and Africa leaders were not even invited to participate.Asiwaju (1985)notes that the partitioning was “essentially a European affair” and Lord Salisbury, the then prime minister of Britain, said “[w]e have been engaged in drawing lines upon maps where no white man’s feet have ever tord; we have been giving away mountains and rivers and lakes to each other, only hindered by the small impediment that we never knew exactly where the mountains and rivers and lakes were.” In another influential work,Alesina et al. (2011)show that as much as 80% of African borders follow latitudinal and longitudinal lines, more than in any other part of the world. This empirical setting enables us to hold geo-climate conditions and ethnic-specific cultural traits constant when assessing the link between colonial legacy and informal finance. In our within-country analyses, as we elaborate in more detail later, we can even net out all the contemporary national institutions and other characteristics to isolate the impact of colonial legacy on informal financial development. This strategy eliminates the omitted variable bias at the country level, where legal origin normally varies. We further exploit the unique history of Cameroon, which has resulted in a setting where we can compare the same control group (i.e., regions in bordering Nigeria, a former British colony) to two otherwise similar groups with different levels of treatment (i.e., places in the historically British- and French-colonized Cameroon), which adds additional confidence to our causal interpretation of the results. 4.1. Graphical Evidence 4.1.1. Cross-Country Graphical Evidence. We start by presenting cross-country graphical evidence on the impact of colonial legacy on the use of informal finance in Africa, using data from the Global Findex database. As shown inFigure 2, British colonial legacy is associated with a higher share of respondents who reportedly use savings clubs than those in areas with a French colonial legacy, across all income quintiles. When we include respondents’ demographic characteristics, such as age, age squared, gender, levels of income, and education, the magnitude of the effect is even larger. We tabulate this set of results in the Online Appendix, Table A2. Although the Global Findex database covers all African countries, it does not provide information on respondents’ subnational locations, preventing us from credibly controlling for omitted variables. 4.1.2. Within-Cameroon Graphical Evidence. To eliminate the country-level omitted variable concerns, we exploit a unique setting within Cameroon. Cameroon was a German colony, known as Kamerun, from 1884 until Britain and France invaded the region in 1916, during WWI, and rapidly divided the country into two administrative regions. The border between the two regions was drawn up hastily and the decision on the division was part of a larger British plan for the future of all other German colonies in Africa. The British thus allowed the French to take the majority of Cameroon (Louis 1967,Brownlie 1979,Ngoh 1988,Dupraz 2019). The division was finalized in 1919 subject to some minor modifications. In 1961, when Cameroon gained independence, the two parts of the country reunited. As the country was striving for complete political and legal unification, a unitary legal system that heavily relies on French institutions has been created in 1972 and continued to operate today. We exploit the historical boundary that divided Cameroon between the British and the French here. This setting allows us to hold national characteristics constant when studying the impact of colonial legacy on informal finance, thus eliminating the country-level omitted variable bias. We start by presenting graphical evidence of the relationship between colonial legacy and informal finance. FollowingBarone et al. (2015)andGuo and An (2022), we first depict the overall cloud of the raw individual observations near the border inFigure 3and show how their distribution behaves as they move farther away from the border using a kernel-weighted local polynomial function. As seen from the raw data, there is a clear discontinuity at the border, suggesting that the British colonial legacy has a positive impact on informal finance usage. InFigure 4, we plot the two-dimensional binned visualization of the same relationship, using the distance from the survey location to the border (the first-order polynomial) as a running variable. Moving from left to right (i.e., from the French side to the British side), we observe a sharp jump in the use of informal finance.16 We next present the three-dimensional evidence across the 1919 border within Cameroon followingDell (2010)to provide a more transparent, clear visualization of the empirical pattern found in the cross-country and within-country two-dimensional (2D) graphical evidence. In particular, we map the respondents’ locations from FinMark Trust, using manually collected coordinates, using the historical border obtained fromGifford and Smith (1967)andDupraz (2019). For every survey location, we calculate the share of respondents who use informal finance and denote it with a colored dot, with darker colors corresponding to higher shares of informal finance users. InFigure 5, we first present the overall distribution of our data in Cameroon, then zoom in on the historical border for a clearer view of the boundary. As shown inFigure 5, there is a sharp jump in the share of informal finance users when moving from the French side of Cameroon to the British side. This 3D visualization of the raw data provides additional evidence that the British colonial legacy in Cameroon is associated with improved informal financial development. 4.2. Regression Analyses 4.2.1. Cross-Country Regression Evidence. To push for a causal interpretation of the link, we leverage a new database, FinMark Trust, which provides information on survey locations and enables us to focus on respondents living close to national borders. Focusing on this border sample allows us to alleviate omitted variable bias that has long plagued cross-country studies on colonial legacy and financial development.17As stressed byMichalopoulos and Papaioannou (2014,2016), many ethnic homelands were partitioned by the arbitrarily designed national borders during the Scramble for Africa that outlived the colonial era. These partitioned ethnic groups present a unique opportunity for us to include ethnic homeland fixed effects when analyzing the border sample. Because respondents from the same ethnic group typically share cultural and social norms, we can mitigate the omitted variable bias arising from these unobservable characteristics. Additionally, in line with the literature, we also include 0.5° × 0.5° grid cell fixed effects in our model, which can help control for geo-climatic variations that might influence our results. Specifically, we use the following regression specification: We report our results inTable 3. As shown in the table,British,is strongly and positively correlated with informal financial development today. Across all specifications,Britishenters the regression with a positive and statistically significant coefficient at the 1% level, and the estimated coefficients are economically large. Consider the estimate from column (3), for example, where we include the full set of controls and fixed effects. This suggests that if an average group of respondents were to move from a former French colony to a former British colony, although remaining within the same ethnic homeland and 0.5° × 0.5° grid cell, they would experience a 16.7-percentage-point increase in the likelihood of being included in an informal finance group. This represents a 30% increase when evaluated at the sample mean (55%). Table 3.Colonial Legacy and Informal Financial Development: Evidence from Cross-Country Borders in Africa Table 3.Colonial Legacy and Informal Financial Development: Evidence from Cross-Country Borders in Africa Notes.This table reports the OLS regression results relating informal financial development to the British, relative to the French, colonial legacy at the country borders. The dependent variable isInformal finance, which is one if the respondent is using informal financial services (i.e., saving, credit, insurance and remittance) and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variable,British, equals one if the respondent is from a former British colony and zero otherwise.Individual controlsinclude a polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (ruralvsurban).Education fixed effectsincludes seven indicators of the level of education, andIncome category fixed effectscontains four indicators of income quantiles. Ethnic group, grid cell (0.5° × 0.5°) and year fixed effects are flexibly included in the regressions. Data are obtained from the FinMark Trust andDjankov et al. (2007). Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at both the ethnic group and grid cell levels. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. To compare the economic magnitude of theBritishvariable against that of important personal characteristics such asEducationandIncome, we reproduce the full results ofTable 3, column (3), in the Online Appendix, Table A4, using standardized coefficients. We find that the impact of living in a place with the British colonial legacy, as opposed to residing in a place with a French colonial legacy, on informal finance is roughly 1.6 times the size of the effect from elevating an average resident’s education level from “Informal school only” to “Secondary school completed.” When comparing the relative impact of the British variable with the effect of Income on informal finance usage, the results suggest that the impact of the British colonial legacy on informal finance is at least twice that of moving from “No income” to “Middle-income” status. Both interpretations suggest that the economic magnitude of the colonial legacy is substantial.19 Although we include ethnic group and grid cell fixed effects in our cross-country border analysis, our results could still be tainted by potential omitted variables arising from country-level characteristics, such as institutions, politics, and nationwide policies. For example, if certain country characteristics are determinants of British colonization and at the same time are conducive to informal financial development, then failing to include these characteristics would bias our results upward. We address this concern in the next subsection. 4.2.2. Spatial RDDs Evidence from Within Cameroon. Because the British colonial legacy treatment is a deterministic and discontinuous function of known covariates—longitude and latitude (or distance to boundary in our robustness checks)—it warrants using a spatial regression discontinuity approach (Dell 2010). In this section, we perform a series of spatial RDDs to further estimate the causal impact of British colonial legacy on informal financial development. Following the seminal work ofDell (2010), we first show visually that several important variables, including agricultural suitability, rainfall, temperature, population density, coverage of forests, mountains, gold mines, and oil fields, electricity grids, and primary road, distance to coast, and night light density change smoothly around the border (see the Online Appendix, Figure A3).20We also perform a McCrary density test on survey locations around the border. The log difference in density at the border is 0.16, with a standard error of 0.29, suggesting no bunching on either side of the border. We then conduct spatial RDDs using the following specification: Our results are reported inTable 4. Columns (1)–(3) focus on respondents who live within ±100 km of the border, whereas columns (4) and (5) further restrict the sample to those located within ±75 and 50 km of the border. Across all specifications,Britishis strongly, positively associated with the probability of being included in the informal financial system. The coefficients onBritishare all statistically significant at the 1% level and very stable when we move from the 100-km band around the border to the narrower 50-km buffer zone. In addition, the estimated coefficients are economically large. For example, consider the estimate in column (5). This suggests that moving a group of respondents from the French-colonized side of Cameroon to the British-ruled side, within the same ethnic homeland and 0.5° × 0.5° grid cell, would lead to a 40.4-percentage-point increase in the probability of using informal finance, representing a 73% increase when evaluated at the sample mean (55%).22 Table 4.Colonial Legacy and Informal Financial Development: Evidence from Cameroon Table 4.Colonial Legacy and Informal Financial Development: Evidence from Cameroon Notes.This table reports the spatial RDD results linking informal financial development to the British, relative to the French, colonial legacy. The dependent variable isInformal finance, which is one if the respondent is using informal financial services (i.e., saving, credit, insurance and remittance) and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variable,British, equals one if the respondent is from the side of Cameroon that was colonized by the British and zero otherwise. Running variables include second-order polynomials of the village coordinates (i.e., longitudes and latitudes).Individual controlsinclude a polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (ruralvsurban).Income category fixed effectsincludes four indicators representing the four quantiles of within-country income distribution.Education fixed effectscontains six indicators representing different levels of education.Ethnic group fixed effectsandGrid cell (0.5° × 0.5°) fixed effectsare included in columns (2) to (5). Columns (1) to (3) present results from respondents situated within 100 km of the border. Columns (4) and (5) further restrict the analysis to respondents located within 75 and 50 km of the border. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at both the ethnic group and grid cell levels. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. 4.2.3. Alternative Bandwidths, RD Polynomials, Clustering Strategies, and Boundary. We perform a plethora of robustness checks on the baseline estimates within Cameroon. First, we gradually restrict our sample from ±100 km to ±10 km around the border, at a 10-km interval, and re-estimate Model (2). We present the results inTable 5. As shown in Panel A, the coefficients ofBritishacross 10 alternative bandwidths are all large, positive, and statistically significant at the 1% level. In addition, the estimates are very stable, suggesting that our results are not driven by respondents at specific locations. Table 5.Robustness Tests on Within Cameroon Spatial RDD Analysis Table 5.Robustness Tests on Within Cameroon Spatial RDD Analysis Notes.This table reports the robustness tests on the within-Cameroon spatial RDD analysis. Panel A uses alternative bandwidths, Panel B presents results with alternative polynomials and alternative running variables (distance to boundary), whereas Panel C employs various alternative clustering strategies. The dependent variable isInformal finance, which is one if the respondent is using informal financial services (i.e., saving, credit, insurance and remittance) and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variableBritish, equals one if the respondent is from the side of Cameroon that was colonized by the British and zero otherwise. Running variables include second-order polynomials of the village coordinates.Individual controlsinclude a polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (ruralvsurban).Income category fixed effectsincludes four indicators representing the four quantiles of within-country income distribution.Education fixed effectscontains six indicators representing different levels of education.Ethnic group fixed effectsandGrid cell (0.5° × 0.5°) fixed effectsare included in all models. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at both the ethnic group and grid cell levels, unless otherwise stated. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. Next, we present results from using various alternative RD polynomials, including linear and cubic polynomials of location coordinates, as well as linear, quadratic, and cubic polynomials of distance to the historical border. We present this set of results in Panel B ofTable 5. As shown in the panel, our coefficients are robust to the use of all these alternative or no polynomials and remain very stable. This analysis suggests that our results are not sensitive to our RD specification. In addition, we employ various alternative clustering strategies to test the sensitivity of our results to the way in which standard errors are calculated. We first experiment with standard errors clustered at the ethnic group, grid cell level, and with no clustering at all. We further calculate the standard errors followingConley (1999)to account for spatial correlation with cutoffs of 10° latitude and 10° longitude. As presented in Panel C ofTable 5, our coefficients remain statistically significant at the 1% level. Finally, we perform an exercise in which we randomly draw the border within the ±20-km buffer of the original boundary 1,000 times and rerun Model (2). We then plot the probability density function of the placebo coefficients and place our original estimate in the figure. As shown inFigure 6, our true estimate lies in the far-right corner of the graph (at 0.404), whereas the distribution of the placebo coefficients is centered around zero, with the vast majority lying within ±0.1. This result implies that our findings are not solely driven by potential measurement errors associated with the historical border. 4.2.4. Addressing Selective Sorting on Informal Finance Around the Border. Although the within-Cameroon analysis is unlikely to be confounded by country level–omitted variables, it could be tainted by selective sorting on informal finance around the border. For instance, people with a higher propensity to use informal finance might move from the French side of Cameroon to the British side or vice versa for reasons that are not observable. If the reason for moving is that the British side is endowed with a favorable legal culture and a style of social control of economic life that supports private market outcomes, including informal finance, then our interpretation of the positive impact of the British colonial legacy on informal finance remains unchanged. The estimated coefficients, however, could be biased upward. Conversely, if the reason for moving is related to factors orthogonal to informal finance, the style of social control, and legal culture, then the results we observe could be spurious. We conduct three sets of analyses to mitigate the possibility that the within-Cameroon estimates are driven by cross-border migrations within Cameroon. Before discussing the tests, it is important to note that the stable and strong link between people’s ethnic identity and basic production factors (e.g., land, water) associated with their identity substantially limits the chance of cross-ethnic homeland migration (Ali et al. 2019). Therefore, our primary concern is with migrations that occur within the same ethnic group but across historical borders. First, because of the high costs of transportation in inland Africa, we conjecture that much of the selective migration will be limited to a rather narrow band of the border. We therefore exclude respondents living within ±10 km of the border and reconduct our analysis. The results remain robust and, if anything, become even stronger. They are tabulated inTable 6, Panel A. Table 6.Addressing Selective Migration Within Cameroon Table 6.Addressing Selective Migration Within Cameroon Notes.This table reports additional robustness tests on the within Cameroon spatial RDD analysis. Panel A presents the results after excluding observations that fall within 10 km of the boundary, whereas Panel B shows results after conservatively adjusting cross-border migration. The dependent variable isInformal finance, which is one if the respondent is using informal financial services (i.e., saving, credit, insurance and remittance) and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variable,British, equals one if the respondent is from the side of Cameroon that was colonized by the British and zero otherwise. Running variables include second-order polynomials of the village coordinates.Individual controlsinclude a polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (rural vs. urban).Income category fixed effectsincludes four indicators representing the four quantiles of within-country income distribution.Education fixed effectscontains six indicators representing different levels of education.Ethnic group fixed effectsandGrid cell (0.5° × 0.5°) fixed effectsare included in all models. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at both the ethnic group and grid cell levels. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. Next, followingDell (2010), we perform an exercise that conservatively takes selective migration into account. In a recent study,Dupraz (2019)uses various rounds of census data in Cameroon to estimate that within-country cross-border in-and-out migration (relative to the British side) is around 4%–9%.23We conservatively recode 10% of the informal finance users on the British-colonized side to nonusers and vice versa on the French-controlled side in Cameroon and rerun Model (2). Note that this exercise attributes cross-border migration fully to selective sorting on informal finance. Panel B inTable 6presents the results. The coefficients, compared with our baseline estimates, are smaller in size but remain statistically significant at the 1% level. This suggests that the positive impact of the British colonial legacy on informal finance is not solely driven by selective migration. 4.3. Three-Group Comparison at the Nigeria-Cameroon BHorder The Nigeria-Cameroon border presents a unique natural experiment that enables us to perform a three-group comparison with two different treatment arms. Particularly, because of Cameroon’s unique colonial history, one part of the Nigeria-Cameroon border connects Nigeria and French Cameroon, whereas the other adjoins Nigeria and British Cameroon. This is conceptually similar to a randomized controlled trial where researchers randomly vary the level of treatment exposure across two treatment groups to better isolate the causal impact (Duflo and Banerjee 2017). In other words, we can better identify the causal impact of colonial legacy by simultaneously comparing informal finance outcomes in regions that have always been under British control (i.e., Nigeria) to those in (a) places that have always been under French control (i.e., French-colonized Cameroon) and (b) places that were initially under British rule but then transferred to French rule because of unification (i.e., British-colonized Cameroon). Respondents in French-controlled Cameroon have had more exposure to French colonial treatment, whereas respondents in British-controlled Cameroon have had less, because they were under the control of British colonizers for about 40 years (1919–1960). If our indicator of British colonial legacy captures the impact of other factors influencing the differential trajectories of informal financial development in Nigeria and Cameroon, then we would expect to see similar results when analyzing the two parts of the border separately. If, however, our analysis captures the true impact of the British colonial legacy, we expect some heterogeneity in the estimates, that is, a smaller effect of the Nigeria-British Cameroon border. InFigure 7, we provide a visualization of the test. We calculate the average share of respondents who use informal finance for each location denoted by a colored dot. Darker colors correspond to higher shares of informal finance users. As we move from French Cameroon to Nigeria (across the upper border), we observe a sharp jump in the share of informal finance users. In contrast, as we move from British Cameroon to Nigeria (across the lower border), the jump (contrast of color) is much milder. In sum, this graphical representation of the raw data suggests that the impact we capture around the Nigeria-Cameroon border is unlikely a reflection of variables other than colonial legacy. We tabulate the results of the spatial RDDs using Model (2) at the border that connects Nigeria and British Cameroon inTable 7, Panel B. In columns (1)–(3), we use the quadratic function of the location coordinates, whereas in columns (4)–(6), we use the linear RD function. When comparing Panel B with Panel A ofTable 7, where we present the results from the border that connects Nigeria and French Cameroon, the coefficients are substantially smaller in size. The median magnitude of British colonization impact reported in Panel B is only one third the size of those shown in Panel A. This is consistent with our conjecture: We expect to see a much milder effect of colonial legacy around the border dividing Nigeria, a former British colony, and British Cameroon, which was under British control for a limited time period (1919–1960), compared with the impact found across the border adjoining Nigeria and French Cameroon, if our analysis accurately captures the true impact of the British colonial legacy. The level of reduction in the estimated magnitude, about two thirds, is also reasonable, given that during a period of roughly 100 years (1919–2017, when the Cameroon survey was conducted), the British-colonized part of Cameroon was under the influence of French rule for about 60 years (1961–2017). Table 7.Colonial Legacy and Informal Financial Development: Three-Group Comparison Table 7.Colonial Legacy and Informal Financial Development: Three-Group Comparison Notes.This table reports the spatial RDD results of the three-group comparison at the Nigeria-Cameroon border. Panel A shows the results of comparing informal financial outcomes in Nigeria, which has always been a British colony, to those in French Cameroon, which has always been under the French colonial system. Panel B presents the results of comparing informal financial outcomes in Nigeria to those in British Cameroon, which was initially under British control but later switched to French rule. The dependent variable is Informal finance, which is one if the respondent is using informal financial services (i.e., saving, credit, insurance and remittance) and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variable, British, equals one if the respondent is from Nigeria and zero if from Cameroon. Running variables include second-order polynomials of the village coordinates in columns (1) to (3) and first-order polynomials in columns (4) to (6).Individual controlsinclude a polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (rural vs. urban).Income category fixed effectsincludes four indicators representing the four quantiles of within-country income distribution.Education fixed effectscontains six indicators representing different levels of education.Ethnic group fixed effectsandGrid cell (0.5° × 0.5°) fixed effectsare included in all regressions. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at both the ethnic group and grid cell levels. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. In sum, the combined evidence from the cross-country border design and spatial RDDs across and within national boundaries suggests that British colonial institutions have a causal positive impact on the development of informal finance.
article.5. Evidence for Mechanisms: Why does colonial legacy have such a profound influence on the development of informal finance today? Why does the impact remain materially large, even when we focus on within-country comparisons in which all contemporary national characteristics are held constant? We investigate three mechanisms that relate colonial legacy to informal finance, including the information-sharing norms, the legacy of the style of social control, and legal culture. In this section, we provide evidence on these channels from both cross- and within-country analyses whenever the data permit. It is important to note that the channels through which colonial legacy influences informal financial development do not operate in isolation. A better localized information flow that helps reduce ex ante information asymmetry, coupled with a culture that favors private economic interactions, promotes the entry of informal financial contracts. The divide-and-rule approach adopted by the British provides strong, private enforcement of informal contracts, helping to sustain these groups. Without effective social sanctions, imposed by local chiefs to curb moral hazards, informal financial groups are difficult to maintain. Furthermore, a high level of information flow because of frequent private and community interactions in the former British colonies not only mitigates adverse selection but also makes private enforcement more effective, further facilitating the development of informal financial markets. 5.1. Information Flow In this section, we examine the link between colonial legacy and measures of information flow. In particular, we rerun Model (2) with an outcome variable of eitherKeep social tiesorExchange ideas.Exchange ideasis an indicator that equals one if the respondent states that the reason for using informal finance is to exchange ideas, and zero otherwise, whereasKeep social tiesequals one if the respondent states that the reason for using informal finance is to keep social ties, and zero otherwise. We argue that stronger social ties, coupled with a greater propensity to share information, can jointly reflect better information flow. Because the variables are only available within Cameroon, we can only perform within-country RDDs. The results are presented inTable 8. Table 8.Colonial Legacy and Information Flow: Within-Country Evidence Table 8.Colonial Legacy and Information Flow: Within-Country Evidence Notes.This table reports the results relating the British colonial legacy to measures of information flow within Cameroon. The dependent variable is eitherKeep social tiesorExchange ideas. These variables are binary indicators that equal one if the respondent uses informal financial services to keep social ties or exchange ideas, respectively, and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variable,British, equals one if the respondent is from the side of Cameroon that was colonized by the British and zero otherwise.Individual controlsa polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (rural vs. urban);Education fixed effectsincludes seven indicators of the level of education;Income category fixed effectscontains four indicators of income quantiles and 10 indicators of income source.Ethnic group and grid cell(0.5° × 0.5°)fixed effectsare included in all regressions. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at both the ethnic group and grid cell levels. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. AsTable 8demonstrates,Britishis positively associated with both measures. The coefficient onBritishis statistically significant at the 1% level across all regressions. The effect size is also economically substantial. For example, consider the estimate in column (3) where we limit our sample to the 50-km buffer zone around of the border. This implies that moving a group of people from the region previously colonized by the French to the British-colonized part of Cameroon would result in a 21-percentage-point increase in their willingness to share ideas with other members of informal finance groups. This effect is large, given that our sample mean is 0.19. 5.2. Style of Social Control Next, we examine the link between colonial legacy and measures of the style of social control favoring private interactions and enforcement. In particular, we run Model (2) but replace the outcome variable with measures of private interactions, includingSafety net,Trust in informal groups,Attend community meetings,Voluntary associations,Community safety, andTraditional leader. We present the results inTable 9. Detailed definitions of these variables are provided inTable 1andSection 3. Table 9.Colonial Legacy and Private Organization of Community and Social Groups: Within-Country Evidence Table 9.Colonial Legacy and Private Organization of Community and Social Groups: Within-Country Evidence Notes.This table reports the results relating the British colonial legacy to private organization of community and social groups within Cameroon. Panel A provides evidence from data obtained from the Afrobarometer, while Panel B shows evidence from the FinMark Trust. The dependent variable in Panel A is eitherAttend community meetings, Community safety, Voluntary associations,orTraditional leader. The first three are binary indicators that equal one if the respondent attends community meetings, feels safe when walking in the community, or is a member of voluntary associations, and zero otherwise.Traditional leaderequals one if the respondent approves the way traditional leaders have performed their job and zero otherwise. In Panel B, the dependent variable is eitherTrust in informal groupsorSafety net. These variables are binary indicators that equal one if the reason that the respondent uses informal financial services is because of higher trust in informal groups or the safety net that informal finance groups provide, respectively, and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variable,British, equals one if the respondent is from the side of Cameroon that was colonized by the British and zero otherwise.Individual controlsinclude a polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (rural vs. urban);Education fixed effectsincludes seven indicators of the level of education;Living Conditions fixed effectsin Panel A contains five indicators, each representing the respondent’s self-evaluation of his/her current living conditions asVery bad,Fairly bad, Neither good nor bad,Fairly Good, orVery good, respectively.Income category fixed effectsin Panel B includes four indicators representing the four quantiles of within-country income distribution. Ethnic group, grid cell (0.5° × 0.5°) fixed effects are included in all regressions. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at both the ethnic group and grid cell levels. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. As shown inTable 9, the British colonial history is strongly and positively associated with all measures of private economic interactions.British, our key interest variable, enters all regressions with positive, statistically significant coefficients at the 1% level. The economic size of our estimates is large. Consider, for example, the estimate ofSafety netinTable 9, Panel B, column (3). This estimate suggests that if a group of respondents were to move from a former French-colonized region/country to a former British-colonized location in the same ethnic homeland and 0.5° × 0.5° grid cell, the probability of their providing economic insurance to each other (Safety net) would increase by 50.9 percentage points. Given that our sample mean ofSafety netis 0.45, this increase is economically substantial. As a key ingredient of the flexible approach to social control adopted by the British in Africa, the local leader’s rule—providing effective contract enforcement (with the threat of expulsion and abrogation of the rights to access resources)—helps sustain informal financial groups. The evidence presented inTable 9, Panel B, supports this argument. As shown in the table,Britishis strongly and positively associated withTraditional leader, an indicator that equals one if the respondent approves the way the traditional leader has performed their job over the past 12 months and zero otherwise. To illustrate the economic magnitude of our estimates, consider the coefficient onTraditional leaderin column (3), Panel A, ofTable 9. This result suggests that, within the same ethnic homeland and 0.5° × 0.5° grid cell, moving a group of people from French to British Cameroon would increase the probability of making a good impression on local leaders by 21.8 percentage points, a 37% increase when evaluated at the sample mean. The better impression made by leaders among people in the former British colonies today reflects a style of social control administered and enforced by local leaders and respected by the people, adding confidence to our proposed channels. 5.3. Legal Culture We next examine the relationship between colonial legacy and the culture that places high levels of trust and respect in the judiciary or whatLa Porta et al. (2008)call legal culture. In particular, we run Model (2) with as an outcome variable eitherTrust courts of laworCourts make binding decisions.Trust courts of lawis an indicator that equals one if the respondent trusts courts of law and zero otherwise, whereasCourts make binding decisionsis equal to one if the respondent agrees with the statement that courts make binding decisions and zero otherwise. We provide evidence inTable 10.24 Table 10.Colonial Legacy and Legal Cultures: Within-Country Evidence Table 10.Colonial Legacy and Legal Cultures: Within-Country Evidence Notes.This table reports the results linking the British colonial legacy to legal cultures from within Cameroon. The dependent variable is eitherTrust courts of laworCourts make binding decisions.
