They are binary indicators that equal one if the respondent reports trust in courts of law and agrees that courts make binding decisions, and zero otherwise. The key explanatory variable,British, equals one if the respondent is from the side of Cameroon that was colonized by the British and zero otherwise.Individual controlsinclude a polynomial of the respondent’s age (age and age-squared), gender, and the type of residency (rural vs. urban);Education fixed effectsincludes seven indicators of the level of education;Living Conditions fixed effectscontains five indicators, each representing the respondent’s self-evaluation of his/her current living conditions asVery bad,Fairly bad, Neither good nor bad,Fairly Good, orVery good, respectively.Ethnic group and grid cell(0.5° × 0.5°)fixed effectsare included in all regressions. Standard errors in parentheses are clustered at the ethnic group level. *, ** and ***significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. AsTable 10shows, British colonial legacy is strongly, positively associated with both variables.Britishenters all specifications with a positive, statistically significant coefficient, at least at the 5% level. The estimates are also economically meaningful. Take the estimate inTable 10, column (3), for example. The coefficient suggests that if a group of people were to move from a former French colony to a former British colony in the same ethnic homeland and 0.5° × 0.5° grid cell, they would experience an increase of 21 percentage points in the probability of trusting the courts. This is a 30% increase, given that the sample mean ofTrust courts of lawis 0.70. Overall, the evidence suggests that the British colonial legacy is associated with a better legal culture in which people are more likely to trust and respect the judiciary. Taken together, our evidence in this section suggests that the British colonial legacy is associated with a style of social control that favors private market interactions, a better legal culture that places more confidence in the judiciary, and a better level of information flow. These, in turn, jointly promote the development of informal finance.
article.6. Conclusions: This paper examines the causal impact of colonial legacy on the development of informal finance today. We georeference a new household survey and use a novel empirical design, exploiting a historical incident—the artificial drawing of colonial borders that partitioned many ethnicities across multiple African countries—to establish causality. By comparing the outcomes of informal finance across regions in very close proximity, belonging to the same ethnic homeland, but with different legal origins both within- and cross-country, we find that places with a British colonial legacy have better informal financial development today than those with a French colonial history. Further exploring the mechanisms, we discover that, although the British colonial legacy helps cultivate a culture that places a high level of trust and respect in the judiciary, positively impacting the development of informal finance groups, Britain’s flexible approach to social control through local leaders provides strong, private enforcement that sustains these groups. Meanwhile, the high information flow in the former British colonies mitigates adverse selection and makes private enforcement more effective, further promoting the development of informal financial markets. Our paper contributes to the literature on the impact of colonial legacy regarding modern finance. Although insightful, the existing work in this area sheds little light on informal financial development. This fact is surprising, given that nearly 44% of the world’s population is unbanked. Our research fills this gap, demonstrating that the British colonial legacy, which incorporates a common law system and a flexible effective social control style, is also conducive to informal finance. Our results are also relevant to policymakers. For instance, our evidence that a good legal culture, combined with effective private enforcement of contracts, can promote access to finance, is relevant for policy designs and programs aimed at promoting financial inclusion and resilience among the poor.
